elements.sent-2411-001: Consider that $\mathbb{G =(\mathbb{X , \mathbb{Y )$ is a simple, connected graph with $\mathbb{X}$ as the vertex set and $\mathbb{Y}$ as the edge set.}}}
elements.sent-2411-002: The atom-bond connectivity ($ABC$) index is a novel topological index that Estrada introduced in Estrada et al. (1998).
elements.sent-2411-003: It is defined as \begin{equation}A B C(G)=\sum_{xy \in Y(G)} \sqrt{\frac{\zeta_x+\zeta_y-2}{\zeta_x \zeta_y}}\end{equation} where $\zeta_x$ and $\zeta_x$ represent the degrees of the vertices $x$ and $y$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-004: In this work, we explore the behavior of the $A B C$ index for tree graphs.
elements.sent-2411-005: We establish both lower and upper bounds for the $A B C$ index, expressed in terms of the graph's order and its Roman domination number.
elements.sent-2411-006: Additionally, we characterize the tree structures that correspond to these extremal values, offering a deeper understanding of how the Roman domination number ($RDN$) influences the $A B C$ index in tree graphs.
elements.sent-2411-007: Key words: $ABC$ index; Roman domination number, Tree, chemical graph theory, Extremal values.
elements.sent-2411-008: Graph theory is used to represent chemical events in the important topic of chemical graph theory within mathematical chemistry.
elements.sent-2411-009: The chemical sciences have advanced significantly as a result of this strategy.
elements.sent-2411-010: A molecular graph is a fundamental graph where the edges represent bonds between atoms, and the vertices represent the atoms themselves.
elements.sent-2411-011: Hydrogen atoms are often omitted from these representations.
elements.sent-2411-012: According to IUPAC terminology, a topological index is a number associated with the chemical structure.
elements.sent-2411-013: This value is used to establish correlations between different physical and chemical attributes, biological activity, and chemical reactivity, and the chemical structure, see \cite{karelson1996quantum, gutman1972graph, kier2012molecular, kier1986molecular, todeschini2008handbook .}
elements.sent-2411-014: The Randi\'{c connectivity index is one of the most well-known topological indices, supported by a solid mathematical foundation and widely applied in pharmacology and chemistry.}
elements.sent-2411-015: In 1998, Estrada et al. \cite{estrada1998atom introduced the $ABC(\mathbb{G )$ index as a notable alternative to the Randi\'{c index.}}}
elements.sent-2411-016: According to Furtula \cite{furtula2016atom , the $ABC$ index ranks among the leading degree-based molecular descriptors. \begin{equation}\label{equ1} A B C(\mathbb{G})=\sum_{xy \in Y(\mathbb{G})} \sqrt{\frac{\zeta_x+\zeta_y-2}{\zeta_x \zeta_y}}\end{equation} where $Y(\mathbb{G )$ represents the set of edges in the graph $\mathbb{G}$, and $\zeta_x$ and $\zeta_y$ are the degrees of the vertices $x$ and $y$ connected by the edge $xy$.}}
elements.sent-2411-017: The index has been widely used to predict molecular stability, boiling points, and other properties, and has proven to be a valuable tool in QSAR/QSPR studies and drug design.
elements.sent-2411-018: Chen and Das \cite{chen2018solution confirmed the conjecture that the Tur\'{a n graph maximizes the $ABC$ index among graphs with a given chromatic number, resolving a problem posed by Zhang et al. \cite{zhang2016maximum .}}}
elements.sent-2411-019: Zheng et al. \cite{zheng2020bounds established bounds for the general $ABC$ index for connected graphs with fixed maximum degree, characterizing the extremal graphs for specific parameter ranges.}
elements.sent-2411-020: Numerous studies have been conducted on this topological indicator, and research on it is still ongoing, see \cite{chen2012atom, lin2017minimal, vassilev2012minimum, lin2013minimal, chen2012some .}
elements.sent-2411-021: Prior research \cite{das2010comparison, xu2012relationships, das2016comparison has investigated the relations between different topological indices and the $ABC$ index.}
elements.sent-2411-022: Among other notable studies, Das and Trinajsti\'{c \cite{das2010comparison looked at the relationship between the $GA$ and $ABC$ index.}}
elements.sent-2411-023: Xinli Xu \cite{xu2012relationships established correlations between the Harmonic index and several other indices, including the $ABC$ index, Randi\'{c index, and the first Zagreb index, based on the order, size, and number of pendant vertices in the graph.}}
elements.sent-2411-024: Further research was done on the connection between the $ABC$ index and the distance-based variation of the $ABC_{\mathbb{G }$ index by Das et al. \cite{das2016comparison .}}
elements.sent-2411-025: Additionally, they determined which extremal trees reach these limits.
elements.sent-2411-026: In order to determine the structures of extremal graphs, Zhang et al. \cite{zhang2024extremal investigated the extremal limits of the $ABS$ index for trees with certain matching and dominance values.}
elements.sent-2411-027: In order to find graphs with the highest and least values of these indices, Wang et al. \cite{wang2018maximizing looked into extremal multiplicative Zagreb indices in graphs with provided vertices and cut edges.}
elements.sent-2411-028: Jamri et al. \cite{hasni2021randic, ahmad2022minimum established both the extreme values of the $RI(\mathbb{T )$ with a specified $TDN$.}}
elements.sent-2411-029: Most recently, Bermudo et al. \cite{bermudo2024geometric, bermudo2020extremal provided an maximum value for the $GA(\mathbb{T )$, based on their order and $TDN$, as well as both extreme values for the $RI(\mathbb{T )$, based on their order and domination number, and research on the bounds of various topological indices with given parameters is still ongoing, as noted in \cite{li2024greatest, wang2024maximum, noureen2024tricyclic, ahmad2023zagreb .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-030: In this study, we take a look at a simple, uncorrected connected graph $\mathbb{G}$, which has a set of vertices $\mathbb{X}$ and an edge set $\mathbb{Y}$.
elements.sent-2411-031: An edge in graph $\mathbb{G}$ connecting two vertices, $x$ and $y$, is represented by the symbol $xy$.
elements.sent-2411-032: The open neighborhood of any vertex $y \in \mathbb{X}$ is defined as $N(y) = {x \in \mathbb{X \mid xy \in \mathbb{Y }$, whereas $N[y] = N(y) \cup {y}$ indicates the closed neighbor.}}
elements.sent-2411-033: The size of an open neighborhood, $|N(x)|$, around a vertex $x$, denoted by $\zeta_x$, is called its degree.
elements.sent-2411-034: A vertex $x$ is called a leaf if $\zeta_x = 1$.
elements.sent-2411-035: The longest path between any two leaves in a tree is defined as its diameter.
elements.sent-2411-036: A diameter path in a tree $\mathbb{T}$ is denoted by $P_{d+1 = {x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_{d+1 }$, where the path between vertices $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_{d+1}$ reaches this maximum length.}}
elements.sent-2411-037: For a given vertex $y \in \mathbb{X}$, the graph $\mathbb{G - {y}$ is obtained by removing $y$, which results in a new vertex set $\mathbb{X - {y}$ and an edge set $\mathbb{Y - {yx \mid x \in N(y)}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-038: Similarly, for an edge $e \in \mathbb{Y}$, the graph $\mathbb{G - {e}$ retains the original vertex set $\mathbb{X}$ but removes the edge $e$, resulting in the edge set $\mathbb{Y - {e}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-039: For $l$ vertices $x_1, \ldots, x_l$ or edges $e_1, \ldots, e_l$, we define the graph $\mathbb{G - {x_1, \ldots, x_l}$ as $\left(\mathbb{G - {x_1, \ldots, x_{l-1 }\right) - {x_l}$, and similarly, $\mathbb{G - {e_1, \ldots, e_l}$ as $\left(\mathbb{G - {e_1, \ldots, e_{l-1 }\right) - {e_l}$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-040: The path graph $\mathbb{P _n$, and the star graph $\mathbb{S _n$.}}
elements.sent-2411-041: We direct the reader to \cite{west2001introduction for definitions of any other notation and terminology not covered here.}
elements.sent-2411-042: The $RDN$ of a graph $\mathbb{G}$ is the minimum weight of a Roman dominating function on $\mathbb{G}$.
elements.sent-2411-043: An $RDN$ is a function $\aleph: \mathbb{X (\mathbb{G ) \rightarrow {0, 1, 2}$ such that every vertex $y \in \mathbb{X (\mathbb{G )$ with $\aleph(y) = 0$ is adjacent to at least one vertex $x \in \mathbb{X (\mathbb{G )$ where $\aleph(x) = 2$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-044: The weight of $\aleph$ is the sum $\aleph(\mathbb{X ) = \sum_{y \in \mathbb{X (\mathbb{G ) \aleph(y)$ \cite{cockayne2004roman .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-045: Essentially, the RDN ensures that any unguarded vertex (with $\aleph(y) = 0$) is adjacent to a heavily guarded vertex (with $\aleph(x) = 2$), and it is denoted by $\Gamma_R$.
elements.sent-2411-046: We introduce a number of lemmas in this section that will be utilized to demonstrate the primary theorem.

elements.sent-2411-077: This section presents the extremal values of the $ABC$ index of trees in terms of their Roman domination number and order.

elements.sent-2411-142: In this study, we examined the $ABC$ index of tree graphs, focusing on its dependence on the $RDN$ and the tree's order.
elements.sent-2411-143: We established the lower and upper bounds of the $ABC$ index, as detailed in Theorems \ref{them2 and \ref{them3 , demonstrating that these bounds are determined by the tree’s order and $RDN$.}}
elements.sent-2411-144: Specifically, Theorem \ref{them4 shows that $\mathbb{P _{n}$ achieve the minimum $ABC$ index, while Theorem \ref{them5 reveals that $\mathbb{S _{n}$ trees attain the maximum index, illustrating the significant impact of the $RDN$ on these extremal values.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-145: These results enhance the understanding of the relationship between tree parameters and topological indices, providing a basis for future research to explore these interactions in broader graph classes and uncover new connections between the $ABC$ index and other graph invariants.
